cards.title: Building H2O
cards.code:
from threading import Semaphore
from threading import Barrier

class H2O:
    def __init__(self):
        self.h_turn = Semaphore(2)
        self.oxygen_turn = Semaphore(1)
        self.water_barrier = Barrier(3)

    def hydrogen(self, releaseHydrogen: 'Callable[[], None]') -> None:
        self.h_turn.acquire()
        # releaseHydrogen() outputs "H". Do not change or remove this line.
        self.water_barrier.wait()
        releaseHydrogen()
        self.h_turn.release()

    def oxygen(self, releaseOxygen: 'Callable[[], None]') -> None:
        # releaseOxygen() outputs "O". Do not change or remove this line.
        self.oxygen_turn.acquire()
        self.water_barrier.wait()
        releaseOxygen()
        self.oxygen_turn.release()

enriched.insight: Semaphores cap the cohort (2 H, 1 O); Barrier(3) makes them rendezvous, so each group is exactly one 2H+1O molecule. Release AFTER the barrier.

cards.title: Design Bounded Blocking Queue
cards.code:
from collections import deque
from threading import Semaphore, Lock


class BoundedBlockingQueue(object):

    def __init__(self, capacity: int):
        self.empty_spots = Semaphore(capacity)
        self.queue = deque()
        self.filled_spots = Semaphore(0)
        self.lock = Lock()

    def enqueue(self, element: int) -> None:
        self.empty_spots.acquire()
        with self.lock:
            self.queue.append(element)
        self.filled_spots.release()

    def dequeue(self) -> int:
        self.filled_spots.acquire()
        with self.lock:
            value = self.queue.popleft()
        self.empty_spots.release()
        return value

    def size(self) -> int:
        with self.lock:
            return len(self.queue)

enriched.insight: Producer/consumer: empty-slots & filled-slots semaphores gate capacity/availability; a Lock guards the deque. Acquire the semaphore OUTSIDE the lock so you never block while holding it.

cards.title: Fizz Buzz Multithreaded

enriched.insight: One dispatcher (number) walks 1..n and releases exactly one sleeping worker per value, then blocks on a single shared done semaphore. Works because only one worker is ever in flight; permits stay conserved so nothing deadlocks.

cards.title: Print Foobar Alternately
cards.code:
from threading import Event
class FooBar:
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.n = n
        self.foo_printed = Event()
        self.bar_printed = Event()
        self.bar_printed.set()


    def foo(self, printFoo: 'Callable[[], None]') -> None:

        for i in range(self.n):
            self.bar_printed.wait()
            self.bar_printed.clear()
            # printFoo() outputs "foo". Do not change or remove this line.
            printFoo()
            self.foo_printed.set()


    def bar(self, printBar: 'Callable[[], None]') -> None:

        for i in range(self.n):
            self.foo_printed.wait()
            self.foo_printed.clear()
            # printBar() outputs "bar". Do not change or remove this line.
            printBar()
            self.bar_printed.set()

enriched.insight: Two Events as alternating gates; seed exactly one open to pick who starts, then wait-clear-mine / set-theirs each step.

cards.title: Print In Order
cards.code:
from threading import Event
class Foo:
    def __init__(self):
        self.done = (Event(), Event())

    def first(self, printFirst: 'Callable[[], None]') -> None:
        # printFirst() outputs "first". Do not change or remove this line.
        printFirst()
        self.done[0].set()

    def second(self, printSecond: 'Callable[[], None]') -> None:
        self.done[0].wait()
        # printSecond() outputs "second". Do not change or remove this line.
        printSecond()
        self.done[1].set()

    def third(self, printThird: 'Callable[[], None]') -> None:
        self.done[1].wait()
        # printThird() outputs "third". Do not change or remove this line.
        printThird()

enriched.insight: Chain order with two Events: first opens gate 0; second waits gate 0, prints, opens gate 1; third waits gate 1. Each step unlocks the next.

cards.title: Print Zero Even Odd

enriched.insight: A 'driver' (zero) hands the baton to odd/even by parity; Semaphore(0)=closed gate, release() opens it for one waiter.

cards.title: The Dining Philosophers
cards.code:
from threading import Semaphore

# 5 forks, each a Semaphore(1) — a fork can be held by one philosopher at a time.
# Which forks a philosopher needs is fixed by their index (left = i, right = i+1 mod 5).
# The deadlock (all 5 grab their left fork, then wait forever on the right) is broken
# by allowed_to_eat: at most 2 philosophers may enter the fork-taking section at once,
# so a full 5-way circular wait can never form.
class DiningPhilosophers:
    def __init__(self):
        self.forks = [Semaphore(1) for _ in range(5)]
        self.allowed_to_eat = Semaphore(2)

    # call the functions directly to execute, for example, eat()
    # Given a philo, first determine their left and right:
    #   0 -> 0,1   1 -> 1,2   2 -> 2,3   3 -> 3,4   4 -> 4,0
    def wantsToEat(self,
                   philosopher: int,
                   pickLeftFork: 'Callable[[], None]',
                   pickRightFork: 'Callable[[], None]',
                   eat: 'Callable[[], None]',
                   putLeftFork: 'Callable[[], None]',
                   putRightFork: 'Callable[[], None]') -> None:
        left_fork, right_fork = self.getLeftandRightForks(philosopher)
        self.allowed_to_eat.acquire()
        self.forks[left_fork].acquire()
        self.forks[right_fork].acquire()
        pickLeftFork()
        pickRightFork()
        eat()
        putLeftFork()
        putRightFork()
        self.forks[left_fork].release()
        self.forks[right_fork].release()
        self.allowed_to_eat.release()

    # Map philosopher id to fork ids, which match the semaphore indices.
    def getLeftandRightForks(self, philosopher):
        left_fork = philosopher
        right_fork = (philosopher + 1) % 5
        return left_fork, right_fork

enriched.insight: Forks are Semaphore(1) locks; grabbing left-then-right invites a 5-way circular-wait deadlock. An extra Semaphore(2) caps how many enter the fork section, so a full cycle can never form.

cards.title: Traffic Light Controlled Intersection
cards.code:
from threading import Lock

class TrafficLight:
    def __init__(self):
        self.greenroad = 1        # which road currently has the green light
        self.lock = Lock()

    def carArrived(
        self,
        carId: int,                      # ID of the car
        roadId: int,                     # road the car travels on: 1 (A) or 2 (B)
        direction: int,                  # direction of the car
        turnGreen: 'Callable[[], None]', # turn the light green on the car's road
        crossCar: 'Callable[[], None]'   # make the car cross
    ) -> None:
        # The lock makes "check the light, switch if needed, cross" one atomic
        # step, so only one car crosses at a time and no car crosses on red.
        with self.lock:
            if self.greenroad != roadId:
                turnGreen()               # perform the switch, not just record it
                self.greenroad = roadId
            crossCar()

enriched.insight: One Lock makes check-light / switch / cross one atomic step so no car crosses on red. Must call turnGreen() to actually switch the light, not just record greenroad.

cards.title: Web Crawler Multithreaded

enriched.insight: Fan out the slow getUrls calls over a thread pool, but consume executor.map results in the main thread so visited/frontier are mutated single-threaded — parallel I/O, zero shared-state races, no lock needed.

cards.title: Anagram Groups

enriched.insight: Use a hashable canonical signature (frozenset of Counter items) as the hashmap key to group anagrams together.

cards.title: Balanced Binary Tree

enriched.insight: Recomputing subtree height at every node from the top yields quadratic time; a bottom-up pass can do it in O(n).

cards.title: Baseball Game

enriched.insight: Use a stack to track valid scores and apply each operation by peeking or popping the top record.

cards.title: Binary Search

enriched.insight: Repeatedly halve the sorted interval to locate the target in logarithmic time.

cards.title: Binary Tree Diameter
cards.code:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right

class Solution:
    def diameterOfBinaryTree(self, root: Optional[TreeNode]) -> int:
        self.diameter = 0
        self.dfs(root)
        return self.diameter

    def dfs(self, node):
        if not node:
            return 0

        left_diameter = self.dfs(node.left)
        right_diameter = self.dfs(node.right)
        curr_diameter = left_diameter + right_diameter
        self.diameter = max(self.diameter, curr_diameter)
        return 1 + max(left_diameter, right_diameter)

enriched.insight: Diameter through a node is left height plus right height; track the global max during a post-order DFS.

cards.title: Binary Tree Inorder Traversal

enriched.insight: Inorder = left, root, right; recursion naturally follows this order using the call stack.

cards.title: Binary Tree Right Side View
cards.code:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right

# id is that person can only see the tail of each level output
# so walk the tree, and get the outtermost
from collections import deque
class Solution:
    def rightSideView(self, root: Optional[TreeNode]) -> List[int]:
        q = deque()
        if root:
            q.append(root)
        output = []

        while q:
            level_observable = []
            for i in range(len(q)):
                node = q.popleft()
                level_observable.append(node.val)
                if node.left:
                    q.append(node.left)
                if node.right:
                    q.append(node.right)

            output.append(level_observable[-1])
        return output

enriched.insight: The rightmost node at each level is visible from the right side, so BFS and take the last element per level.

cards.title: Climbing Stairs

enriched.insight: Memoize the Fibonacci recurrence so each step is computed once instead of exponentially many times.

cards.title: Depth Of Binary Tree

enriched.insight: Tree depth equals 1 plus the max depth of its left and right subtrees; recursion handles this naturally.

cards.title: Design Word Search Data Structure

enriched.insight: Store words in a trie and backtrack through all child branches when a '.' wildcard is encountered during search.

cards.title: Duplicate Integer

enriched.insight: Use a hashmap to count occurrences and check if any count exceeds one.

cards.title: Eating Bananas

enriched.insight: Binary search the minimum eating speed, validating with total hours via ceiling division.

cards.title: Find Pivot Index

enriched.insight: Use prefix sums to compare left and right subarray sums in constant time at each index.

cards.title: Implement Prefix Tree

enriched.insight: Trie nodes store children in a hashmap and a word-end flag, supporting insert, search, and prefix checks in O(length) time.

cards.title: Insert Into A Binary Search Tree

enriched.insight: Recursively traverse using BST ordering until reaching a null child, then attach the new node there.

cards.title: Invert A Binary Tree
cards.code:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right

class Solution:
    def invertTree(self, root: Optional[TreeNode]) -> Optional[TreeNode]:
        if root is None:
            return None
        original_left = root.left
        root.left = root.right
        root.right = original_left
        root.left = self.invertTree(root.left)
        root.right = self.invertTree(root.right)
        return root

enriched.insight: Swap each node's left and right children, then recursively invert the new left and right subtrees.

cards.title: Is Anagram

enriched.insight: Two strings are anagrams iff their character frequency counts are identical.

cards.title: Is Palindrome

enriched.insight: Skip non-alphanumeric chars from both ends and compare lowercase letters inward.

cards.title: Level Order Traversal Of Binary Tree
cards.code:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right
from collections import deque
class Solution:
    def levelOrder(self, root: Optional[TreeNode]) -> List[List[int]]:
        output = []
        q = deque()
        
        if root:
            q.append(root)



        while q:
            level_output = []
            for i in range(len(q)):
                
                curr_node = q.popleft()
                level_output.append(curr_node.val)
                if curr_node.left:
                    q.append(curr_node.left)
                if curr_node.right:
                    q.append(curr_node.right)

            output.append(level_output)
        return output

enriched.insight: Process each queue level in a fixed-size inner loop to group values by tree depth.

cards.title: Minimum Stack

enriched.insight: Keep a parallel min-stack storing the minimum seen so far at each push.

cards.title: Partition Equal Subset Sum

enriched.insight: Partitioning is possible iff some subset sums to exactly half the total sum.

cards.title: Path Sum

enriched.insight: Track remaining target at each node; a leaf with zero remainder means a valid root-to-leaf path exists.

cards.title: Range Sum Query Immutable
cards.code:
class NumArray:

    def __init__(self, nums: List[int]):
        self.nums = nums
        self.prefixSums = []
        
        prefix = 0
        for num in nums:
            prefix += num
            self.prefixSums.append(prefix)
        


    def sumRange(self, left: int, right: int) -> int:
        rightSum = self.prefixSums[right]
        leftSum = 0
        if left > 0:
            leftSum = self.prefixSums[left - 1]
        return rightSum - leftSum

# Your NumArray object will be instantiated and called as such:
# obj = NumArray(nums)
# param_1 = obj.sumRange(left,right)

enriched.insight: Precompute cumulative sums so any range sum is a constant-time subtraction of two prefix sums.

cards.title: Reverse A Linked List
cards.code:
# Definition for singly-linked list.
# class ListNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
#         self.val = val
#         self.next = next

class Solution:
    def reverseList(self, head: Optional[ListNode]) -> Optional[ListNode]:
        if not head:
            return None
        
        nextHead = head
        if head.next:
            nextHead = self.reverseList(head.next)
            head.next.next = head
        head.next = None
        return nextHead

enriched.insight: Recursively reverse the tail, then point the next node back to current and sever current's forward link.

cards.title: Search 2D Matrix

enriched.insight: Binary search the row range first, then binary search within the chosen row.

cards.title: String Encode And Decode

enriched.insight: Prefix each string with its length and a delimiter to allow unambiguous decoding.

cards.title: Subarray Sum Equals K

enriched.insight: Count previous prefix sums equal to current prefix sum minus k to find valid subarrays ending at each index.

cards.title: Three Integer Sum

enriched.insight: Sort the array, then for each first element use two pointers to find pairs that complete a zero sum, skipping duplicates.

cards.title: Top K Elements In List

enriched.insight: Count frequencies with a hashmap, sort keys by frequency descending, then return the first k elements.

cards.title: Two Integer Sum

enriched.insight: Store each number's index in a hashmap so you can instantly find the complement when you reach it.

cards.title: Step A — API / Interface
cards.front: How do you approach the API step?

recall: One endpoint per functional requirement — no more
REST for CRUD-ish systems, RPC for action-y ones
Note pagination (cursor), streaming, or websockets where relevant
Show a reasonable interface, then MOVE ON — don't gold-plate

cards.title: Step D — Deep Dives
cards.front: What do Deep Dives cover, and why do they matter?

recall: Go back to the NON-functional list and defend each item
Scaling a hot component, caching, partitioning/sharding, a queue for spikes
Consistency handling, a specific algorithm choice, failure/edge cases
This is where senior / staff signal is earned

cards.title: Step C — Core Entities
cards.front: What is the Core Entities step for, and how long?

recall: Name the handful of nouns your system centers on (User, Tweet, Ride, File...)
They become your data model and the payloads your API returns
A bulleted list is enough — do NOT design the schema yet
Gives you shared vocabulary with the interviewer before you build

cards.title: Step H — High-Level Design
cards.front: What is the goal of the High-Level Design step?

recall: Minimum components that make EVERY functional requirement work end-to-end
Typical spine: client -> gateway/LB -> service -> DB / cache / queue / blob store
Trace one request all the way through the boxes
Breadth first — resist deep-diving here

cards.title: Non-functional requirements checklist
cards.front: Which non-functional dimensions do you scan in every question?

recall: Scale — reads/writes per second, data volume, fan-out
Latency — what must feel instant (reads) vs what can lag (writes/analytics)
Consistency vs Availability — where is stale data OK vs not (CAP)
Durability — can we ever lose this data?
Read:write ratio — usually read-heavy; decides where caches/replicas go
Pick the 3-4 that actually bite THIS system; don't list all of them

cards.title: The R-C-A-H-D framework
cards.front: Recall the 5 steps of the delivery framework and their time budgets.

recall: R — Requirements (~5m): functional + non-functional + capacity estimate
C — Core Entities (~2m): the nouns your API & DB use
A — API / Interface (~5m): one endpoint per functional requirement
(Data Flow ~5m: optional, only for data-pipeline questions)
H — High-Level Design (~10-15m): boxes & arrows for the functional reqs
D — Deep Dives (~10m): defend the non-functional reqs & bottlenecks

cards.title: Step R — Requirements
cards.front: What three things do you produce in the Requirements step, and how long?

recall: Functional: 'Users should be able to...' — the 2-3 CORE features only, park the rest as out-of-scope
Non-functional (the -ilities): scale, latency, consistency vs availability (CAP), durability, read:write ratio
Capacity estimate: only numbers that CHANGE a decision (e.g. 5 TB/day -> partition; 1M RPS -> CDN+cache)
This list is the contract your Deep Dives will defend later

cards.title: Min Max Shortcut
cards.code:
from typing import List


def disallow_negatives(num: int) -> int:
    return max(0, num)


def max_difference(nums: List[int]) -> int:
    diff = 0
    for i in range(len(nums) - 1):
        diff = max(nums[i + 1] - nums[i], diff)
    return diff





# do not modify below this line
print(disallow_negatives(-2))
print(disallow_negatives(-1))
print(disallow_negatives(0))
print(disallow_negatives(1))
print(disallow_negatives(2))

print(max_difference([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]))
print(max_difference([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9]))
print(max_difference([10, 1, 3, 7]))
print(max_difference([2, 4, 7, 5, 7, 8, 4, 2]))

enriched.insight: Use max(0, x) to floor negatives and track the largest adjacent difference in a single pass.

cards.title: Sort Custom

enriched.insight: Pass a key function to sorted() to order elements by a derived value without mutating the input list.

cards.title: Sort Descending

enriched.insight: Python's sorted() with reverse=True returns a new list in descending order using Timsort.

cards.title: Sort Lambda

enriched.insight: Use sorted with a key lambda to order elements by a derived value without changing the original list.

cards.title: Sorted Copy
cards.code:
from typing import List


def sort_words(words: List[str]) -> List[str]:
    output = sorted(words)
    return output


def sort_numbers(numbers: List[int]) -> List[int]:
    output = sorted(numbers, reverse=True, key=abs)
    return output


# do not modify below this line
original_words = ["cherry", "apple", "blueberry", "banana", "watermelon", "zucchini", "kiwi", "pear"]

print(original_words)
print(sort_words(original_words))

original_numbers = [1, -5, -3, 2, 4, 11, -19, 9, -2, 5, -6, 7, -4, 2, 6]

print(original_numbers)
print(sort_numbers(original_numbers))

enriched.insight: Use sorted with key and reverse to return a new sorted list while leaving the original unchanged.

cards.title: Unpacking
cards.code:
from typing import List, Tuple


def sum_3_integers(triplet: List[int]) -> int:
    a, b, c = triplet
    return a + b + c


def compute_volume(box_dimensions: Tuple[int, int, int]) -> int:
    x, y, z = box_dimensions
    return x * y * z
  

# do not modify below this line
print(sum_3_integers([1, 2, 3]))
print(sum_3_integers([4, 6, 2]))

print(compute_volume((1, 2, 3)))
print(compute_volume((3, 2, 1)))
print(compute_volume((3, 9, 7)))

enriched.insight: Python unpacking binds sequence elements to named variables in one readable statement.

cards.title: Design an Ad Click Aggregator
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for aggregating ad-click metrics.

functional: Ingest ad-click events at high volume
Query aggregated metrics (clicks per ad over time windows)
nonfunctional: Very high write throughput; near-real-time analytics
Durability of raw events; approximate is OK for fast dashboards
This is a data-pipeline question — add a Data Flow
hld: Clicks -> ingestion service -> stream (Kafka)
Stream processor (Flink/Spark) aggregates into time buckets
Aggregates written to an OLAP store; queried by dashboards
deepDives: Two paths (lambda-ish): fast approximate stream aggregation for live dashboards + a batch job over raw events for exact/corrected numbers
Windowing: tumbling windows (e.g. per-minute) keyed by adId; handle late events with a grace period
Scale writes: partition the stream by adId; keep raw events in cheap durable storage for reprocessing

cards.title: Design a Chat App (WhatsApp / Messenger)
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for real-time 1:1 and group chat.

functional: Send/receive messages in real time (1:1 and group)
Delivery & read receipts
Deliver messages sent while a user is offline
nonfunctional: Low latency for delivery; ordered per conversation
High availability; messages must not be lost (durable)
Huge concurrent connection count
api: WebSocket: send(convId, text); server pushes incoming messages
hld: Clients hold persistent WebSocket to a Chat/Gateway server
A registry (Redis) maps userId -> which server holds their connection
Message service persists then routes to the recipient's server; offline -> stored for pull on reconnect
deepDives: Connection routing at scale: pub/sub (Redis/Kafka) between gateway servers so any server can reach any online user
Ordering & delivery: per-conversation sequence numbers; ACKs drive sent/delivered/read state
Offline delivery: durable message store + per-user 'last read' offset; sync on reconnect. Group fan-out writes to each member's inbox

cards.title: Design Dropbox / Google Drive
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for file storage & sync.

functional: Upload / download files
Sync files across a user's devices
Share files with other users
nonfunctional: Durable — never lose a file; highly available
Large files (GBs); efficient sync of small changes
Read-heavy (downloads)
hld: Metadata service (DB) separate from blob storage (S3) for the bytes
Client uploads chunks directly to blob store via presigned URLs
Sync: clients poll/subscribe to a changes feed keyed on metadata versions
deepDives: Chunking: split files into fixed chunks, hash each; only upload/download changed chunks (dedup + fast sync)
Upload path: presigned S3 URLs so bytes bypass app servers; metadata committed after chunks land
Sync & conflicts: per-file version vectors; CDN for downloads; large files use multipart upload

cards.title: Design a Leaderboard / Top-K
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for a real-time ranked leaderboard.

functional: Update a player's score
Get top-K players and a player's own rank
nonfunctional: Low-latency reads of ranks; frequent score updates
Real-time-ish; huge number of players
hld: Redis Sorted Set (ZSET) per leaderboard: score-ordered, O(log n) update, O(log n + k) top-k
Writes update the ZSET; reads query ranks directly
Durable copy of scores in a DB behind the cache
deepDives: Sorted set gives rank & top-k cheaply; ZADD to update, ZREVRANGE for top-k, ZREVRANK for a player's rank
Scale: shard leaderboards by game/region; for one giant board, partition by score range and merge top-k across shards
Time windows (daily/weekly): separate ZSETs with TTL; persist to DB for history

functional: Send notifications across channels (push, SMS, email)
Support user preferences and templates
nonfunctional: High throughput, spiky load; at-least-once delivery
Decoupled from senders; resilient to third-party (APNs/FCM/Twilio) failures
hld: Producers publish notification events to a message queue (Kafka)
Channel workers consume, render templates, call the provider (APNs/FCM/SES/Twilio)
Preference service filters/opt-outs before dispatch
deepDives: Queue decouples spikes from delivery; per-channel worker pools scale independently
Reliability: retries with backoff + dead-letter queue; idempotency keys to avoid duplicate sends
Rate limiting per provider; batching; user-level dedup/throttle so users aren't spammed

cards.title: Design a Distributed Rate Limiter
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for limiting request rates across a fleet.

functional: Allow/deny a request based on a per-client limit (e.g. 100 req/min)
Work consistently across many app servers
nonfunctional: Very low latency (it's in the hot path of every request)
Highly available; approximate limits acceptable under failure
Scales with request volume
hld: Rate-limit check runs at the API gateway / middleware
Shared counter store (Redis) keyed by clientId so all servers agree
On each request: atomically increment & compare to limit
deepDives: Algorithm: token bucket (allows bursts, smooth refill) or sliding-window counter (accurate, avoids fixed-window edge bursts)
Atomicity across servers: Redis INCR/EXPIRE or a Lua script for check-and-increment in one round trip
Failure mode: fail-open (allow) vs fail-closed; local in-memory fallback + async sync to reduce Redis pressure

cards.title: Design Ticketmaster
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for booking event seats.

functional: Browse events and available seats
Reserve/hold a seat, then purchase it
nonfunctional: Strong consistency — never sell the same seat twice
Handle massive spikes (popular on-sales / thundering herd)
Reads (browsing) far outnumber writes (booking)
hld: Booking service with a relational DB (ACID) as source of truth for seat state
Search/browse served from a read-optimized store + cache
Seat hold with a TTL, then confirm on payment
deepDives: No double-booking: reserve a seat with a distributed lock or a DB row lock / status transition (AVAILABLE->HELD->BOOKED) inside a transaction
Holds expire: TTL (Redis key or a reservation row + expiry sweeper) auto-releases unpaid seats
Thundering herd on hot events: a virtual waiting queue admits users at a controlled rate; cache the seat map and update via events

cards.title: Design a URL Shortener (TinyURL)
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for a URL shortener.

functional: Create a short URL from a long URL (optional custom alias & expiry)
Redirect a short URL to the original long URL
nonfunctional: Read-heavy (100:1) — redirects must be low-latency
High availability for redirects; short codes must be unique
Scale: ~1B URLs, redirects at high RPS
hld: Client -> API service -> DB (key-value: shortCode -> longUrl)
Redirect path: read shortCode, fetch longUrl, return 302
Cache hot short codes (Redis) in front of the DB
deepDives: Unique code generation: counter + base62 (short, sequential) vs hash-of-URL (dedupes, needs collision check). Counter via a distributed range service / Redis INCR
Scale reads: CDN + Redis cache; DB read replicas — most redirects never hit primary
Partition the KV store by shortCode; short codes are ~7 base62 chars for ~3.5T combos

cards.title: Design Twitter / a News Feed
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for a social news feed.

functional: Post a tweet
Follow users
View a home timeline of tweets from people you follow
nonfunctional: Read-heavy; timeline load must be fast (<200ms)
Eventual consistency OK (a tweet can appear a few seconds late)
Handle celebrity fan-out (millions of followers)
hld: Write path: Tweet service -> Tweet DB + fan-out service
Fan-out-on-write: push tweetId into each follower's precomputed timeline cache (Redis list)
Read path: GET /feed reads the precomputed list — fast
deepDives: Celebrity problem: hybrid fan-out — push for normal users, but pull (merge at read time) for users who follow celebrities, to avoid millions of writes per tweet
Feed ranking: store tweetIds, hydrate tweet content from cache at read time
Storage: shard tweets by userId; timeline caches in Redis with a bounded length

cards.title: Design Uber (ride-sharing)
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for matching riders to nearby drivers.

functional: Riders request a ride from A to B
Match rider to a nearby available driver
Track driver location live during the trip
nonfunctional: Low latency matching; frequent location updates (write-heavy)
High availability; strong consistency on a match (one driver, one rider)
Geospatial queries at city scale
api: WebSocket for match + live trip updates
hld: Location service ingests high-frequency driver pings into an in-memory geo index
Ride service queries nearby drivers, offers the ride, confirms a match
Notification via websocket to matched driver & rider
deepDives: Geospatial index: geohash or QuadTree / H3 cells; query the rider's cell + neighbors for candidate drivers
Location writes: don't persist every ping to the primary DB — keep current location in Redis, only snapshot periodically
Match consistency: lock/atomic claim so a driver isn't double-booked; timeout & re-offer if a driver doesn't accept

cards.title: Design a Web Crawler
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for crawling the web at scale.

functional: Fetch pages starting from seed URLs
Extract links and keep crawling
Store page content for downstream (e.g. indexing)
nonfunctional: Massive scale (billions of pages); high throughput
Politeness — don't hammer one domain; respect robots.txt
Fault tolerant & resumable
hld: URL frontier (queue) -> fetcher workers -> parser -> store + extract links -> back into frontier
Seen-URL set to avoid re-crawling
Content written to blob store
deepDives: Frontier design: prioritized + per-domain queues with rate limits for politeness
Dedup: bloom filter / hashed URL set for 'have I seen this?' at scale; content hashing for near-duplicate pages
Scale & resilience: partition the frontier by domain hash; workers are stateless & retry; checkpoint progress

cards.title: Design YouTube / Netflix (video streaming)
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for video upload & streaming.

functional: Upload a video
Stream/watch a video (adaptive quality)
nonfunctional: Very read-heavy; smooth playback (low buffering) globally
Durable storage of originals; huge bandwidth
Upload is async / eventually available
hld: Upload -> raw blob store -> async transcoding pipeline (multiple resolutions/bitrates)
Transcoded segments stored in blob store, fronted by a CDN
Player fetches an adaptive-bitrate manifest (HLS/DASH), pulls segments from nearest CDN edge
deepDives: Transcoding: fan-out per (resolution x codec) as parallel jobs via a queue; segment the video so segments transcode independently
Delivery: CDN is the whole game — cache segments at the edge; ABR lets the client drop quality instead of buffering
Metadata & views: view counts via an async aggregation pipeline, not synchronous DB writes
